_doc: Source-grounded extraction from the paper corpus. Each finding carries a short verbatim quote (<=200 chars) and a locator for verification, and is the material behind enrichment/observations.json. Educational resource - not for clinical decision-making.

papers.notes: 116 seizures / 19 unilateral TLE patients (10F/9M, mean 28.7y); all had ATL and were seizure-free 6mo-1yr. 65 seizures NHS (11 pts), 51 DHS (8 pts); 85 left temporal, 31 right. Epileptogenic zone decided by ictal scalp/sphenoidal EEG (+bitemporal depth in 5 pts), MRI, PET, SPECT, Wada; surgical outcome considered. Chi-square used. Dystonia, tonic posture, clonic jerking, head version = strong contralateral signs; unilateral automatism and eye blinking = ipsilateral; ictal normal speech = nondominant, dysphasia = dominant. Cites Kotagal 1989 (unilateral dystonia in 15% of TLE patients) as external figure, not own data.

papers.notes: 35 patients (aged 12-39, mean 24.6), 100 CPS (80 CPS, 20 secondarily generalized); 16 patients/48 seizures dominant onset, 19 patients/52 seizures nondominant. Scalp + chronically implanted subdural electrodes; speech dominance by intracarotid amobarbital (Wada). Only postictal dysphasia (92% dominant, p<0.001) and ictal identifiable speech (83% nondominant, p=0.013) had significant lateralizing value. 97% of patients had speech manifestation in >=1 seizure; 79% of seizures had >=1 speech phenomenon. If 3 isolated-paraphasia patients excluded, all postictal-dysphasia patients had dominant onset. External citations (not own data): Serafetinides & Falconer 67%; McKeever expressive dysphasia/speech arrest in 94%; Hecaen & Piercy 31/63 vs 4/34; Bingley ictal dysphasia 48% of 33; Bingley ictal speech automatisms 39% (29/74), 25% (6/24) dominant vs 59% (10/17) nondominant.

papers.notes: 19 consecutive unilateral TLE patients (all right-handed; Wada left-dominant in 14/16, bilateral in 2/16), RTL 10 pts/54 sz, LTL 9 pts/73 sz. EEG-CCTV scalp+sphenoidal (5-14 days) + subdural grids in 6 dominant-focus patients; surgical outcome per Engel. Three most significant differentiators: postictal aphasia (LTL only), well-formed ictal speech and rapid postictal return to baseline (RTL only). MTS on MRI more common in RTLE (5/10) than LTLE (2/9), NS (imaging, not semiology). External citations: Gabr 1989 ictal speech 12 patients (10 nondominant); Koerner & Laxer ictal speech 13 patients (12 RTLE). Note OCR percent quirks: secondarily-generalized cells read '4 (6)' and '19 (24)'; well-formed speech '17 (33)' and speech-arrest/postictal figures mix seizure counts with 'patient' percentages in the discussion.

papers.cite: Montavont et al. 2005
papers.design: single case report with SEEG + 50 Hz electrical stimulation, right mesial TLE

findings.value_text: 12% to 39% of drug-resistant TLE patients; more frequent in non-dominant temporal lobe onset
findings.quote: Ictal speech automatisms or verbalisations are observed in 12% to 39% of patients with drug resistant temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) ... significantly more frequent in seizures originating from the non-dominant than the dominant temporal lobe.

findings.phenomenon: ictal dysprosody (altered prosody: monotonous, hypophonic, neutral speech)
findings.value_text: single case (n=1); reproduced by localized 50 Hz stimulation of right precentral operculum
findings.quote: the brain regions involved in his dysprosody primarily include the right frontal operculum. ... stimulation of the right precentral operculum induced a ... disruption of prosody while the patient was counting aloud

papers.notes: N=1 case report. 35-year-old right-handed man, right mesial TLE; Wada showed strictly left language lateralisation. 11 SEEG electrodes in right hemisphere + 1 in left amygdala. Spontaneous seizures began right amygdala; dysprosodic stereotyped utterances ("mummy, I love you") appeared as discharge invaded non-dominant precentral (P) and postcentral (E) opercula. 5s/50 Hz/1 mA stimulation of right precentral operculum reproduced dysprosody (faster counting, higher pitch, hypophonia). FDG-PET right amygdala hypometabolism; ictal SPECT right mesial temporal hyperperfusion; right mesial temporal + temporo-polar resection, seizure-free >3 years. Authors propose ictal dysprosody as a possible localizing sign implicating non-dominant frontal operculum; 12-39% figure is cited external literature, not own data.

papers.cite: Loddenkemper et al. 2005 (Epileptic Disorders)
papers.design: methodology/opinion: proposal for a five-dimensional patient-oriented epilepsy classification
papers.notes: This paper proposes a five-dimensional epilepsy classification (epileptogenic zone, semiology, etiology, seizure frequency, related conditions). It contains NO quantitative data on the localizing/lateralizing value of any individual seizure sign, aura, or semiology. The only numbers present concern prevalence of ILAE syndromes in populations (e.g., 1% localization-related and 4% generalized syndromes; 4% of adults and 19% of children with a specific ILAE syndrome) - not sign-level lateralization/localization - so no findings extracted.

papers.notes: Original retrospective single-center cohort (73 patients, 145 seizures), NOT a literature-mined meta. Ground truth = enduring seizure freedom (Engel Ia >=1 yr) after single-lobe resection. Three blinded observers; PPV and Free-Marginal kappa computed. Table 1 gives per-sign PPV for lateralization (individual signs); Tables 2-4 give aggregate semiology values per lobe and vs EEG/MRI/PET. Noted internal discrepancies: FLE 20/27 (text) vs 21/27 (tables); occipital localization 87.5% (Table 3) vs 62.5% (Section 6.2); APR Table 1 row (21(17)) appears duplicated from nose-wiping row while body text gives APR 6/8 seizures in 4/5 patients.

Cohort: 54 patients (22M/32F), mean age 24.9±9.5y, epilepsy duration 16.9±8y; 27/54 (50%) normal MRI. 374 seizures (mean 11, median 7/patient). Sign->completion of semiology 1-12 s (median 4 s); electrical-clinical onset interval 0.5-10 s (median 3 s); shorter with motor/premotor onset, longer with prefrontal onset. Histopath (n=33): 19 (58%) FCD/DNET, 14 (42%) gliosis/other. Rostro-caudal gradient: more anterior organization -> more integrated behavior; distal stereotypies=most anterior prefrontal, proximal stereotypies=more posterior prefrontal. value-test is units of SD from the mean (>2 characteristic present); numeric values transcribed from Table 1 positive/defining signs per group. Mesio-lateral axis was NOT a reliable localizing indicator.

papers.notes: Narrative review; most content qualitative. Central thesis: FLS motor semiology maps onto a rostro-caudal (antero-posterior) hierarchical axis of frontal organization (based largely on Bonini 2014, ref 5). Table 1 gives sublobar structure->semiology mapping (precentral=clonic/simple tonic; premotor=proximal tonic + head/eye version; prefrontal=complex gestural + distal>proximal stereotypies). Notes lack of specificity: hyperkinetic behavior & altered consciousness NOT associated with specific prefrontal localization and occur in extra-frontal seizures. Numeric findings mostly cited from other works (Englot meta-analysis 45% seizure-free; Singh et al in-prep fear ~40%/facial ~20%/threat ~10% in 42 SEEG prefrontal pts).

papers.notes: Landmark SEEG study individually characterizing the Medial-Lateral (ML) temporal subtype for the first time. Subtype figures are frequency of each sign within SEEG-defined M/ML/L onset groups (localization within temporal lobe, not L/R lateralization) - direction coded 'none' with subtype association given in value_text; value populated with the associated subtype percentage only when p<0.05, else null. Summary conclusions: M subtype = initial epigastric/viscerosensory symptoms, fear, dreamy state, long duration, delayed loss of contact and delayed oroalimentary/upper-limb automatisms; L subtype = initial sensory illusion/hallucination (mainly auditory), initial loss of contact, shorter duration, frequent secondary generalizations; ML subtype = shares M subjective symptoms plus early loss of contact and early oroalimentary/verbal/vocal automatisms. Discussion (non-quantitative) assigns Heschl's gyrus to primary auditory hallucinations, STG to complex auditory, temporo-parietal junction to vertigo, basal-temporal/temporo-occipital junction to complex visual. Non-semiologic differentiators (not extracted as findings): febrile seizure history M 58.3%/ML 22.2%/L 0% p=0.0004; hippocampal sclerosis on MRI M 66.7%/ML 22.2%/L 0% p<0.0001; younger onset in M (7.9 vs 12.8 vs 11.2 yr, p=0.021); Engel I outcome no subtype difference (82.6% M/66.6% ML/75% L).

papers.notes: Classic n=100 ATL speech series. All but 3 patients right-handed; left resection presumed dominant hemisphere. Two phenomena distinguished: (A) paroxysmal dysphasia (remembered, consciousness preserved) = strong evidence of dominant-hemisphere onset; (B) ictal speech automatism (linguistically correct utterances with subsequent amnesia) = may arise either hemisphere, slightly more often recessive/nondominant. Only 4 patients showed both types (independent, never in same seizure). Group A (53 seizure-free) treated as high-accuracy localization; Group B (47 continuing fits) less accurate but same trend. Non-lateralizing observations not extracted: >3/4 of 34 dysphasia cases also had grand mal vs <half of automatism cases; half of dysphasia cases had transient post-op nominal dysphasia; only 3/17 Group A dysphasia left with mild persistent nominal dysphasia (5/100 whole series).

papers.notes: Framework paper: semiology is an emergent property of epileptogenic NETWORKS, not a fixed output of a focus. Key claims: (1) A variable time-lag separates electrical onset (fast discharge) from first clinical sign; the 'early spread zone' (not just onset zone) determines semiology and is the organic link between epileptogenic zone and semiology. (2) Discharge FREQUENCY is a crucial determinant: fast/gamma discharge deactivates normal function (negative signs), slower theta can mimic/exacerbate physiological function; in M1 a phasic+fast discharge gives myoclonic jerk while higher-frequency oscillation gives posture/movement arrest. (3) Primary sensory cortex has near-linear/topographic relation to periphery, so elementary hallucinations/illusions are localizing and lateralized (e.g., elementary visual hallucinations in left superior quadrant indicate right infra-calcarine cortex; retinotopic/tonotopic/somatotopic + contralateral organization); more complex visual phenomena (dyschromatopsia, metamorphopsia) imply temporo-occipital associative areas. (4) SMA seizures: tonic asymmetric posturing generally contralateral to discharge; pre-SMA -> speech arrest; negative motor areas (dorsal/ventral premotor) -> inhibitory/negative motor phenomena. (5) 'March' (Bravais/Jackson) rarely observed; requires single-area onset. (6) SYNCHRONIZATION effects: increased theta synchrony hippocampus-rhinal cortex-neocortex underlies deja vu / dreamy state / recollection; ictal humming = increased coherence between STG, planum temporale and inferior frontal gyrus (area 44/45), with concordant ictal-SPECT hyperperfusion. (7) 'RELEASE effect' via functional UNCOUPLING: fear-related hypermotor behavior in fronto-temporal seizures shows massive desynchronization between medio-basal prefrontal cortex and amygdala at fast discharge; jargonaphasia from BTLA via desynchronizing fast discharge. (8) Verbal automatisms associated with NON-dominant hemisphere for language. (9) Critiques 'localizational reductionism' and the fixed 'symptomatogenic zone' concept; semiology depends on dynamic interaction between onset and propagation, not either alone. Only sign-specific numeric figures are the cortical-stimulation percentages in Fig.1 (extracted above).

papers.cite: McGonigal A, Bartolomei F, Chauvel P. On seizure semiology. Epilepsia. 2021;62:2019-2035.
papers.design: Critical Review / Invited Commentary (conceptual framework)
papers.notes: Framework/review. No original sign-specific localizing/lateralizing percentages generated by this paper; Tables 1-2 summarize OTHER SEEG studies (subject/seizure counts given, but findings are qualitative). Core arguments: epilepsy is better modeled as an epileptogenic NETWORK than a focus; specific rhythmic interactions (discharge frequency, lag, synchrony) between structures - not just anatomy - shape clinical expression. Critiques Luders' 'symptomatogenic zone' ("area of cortex that when activated produces the ictal symptoms") as incompatible with network/temporal/subcortical realities. Proposes a SEMIOLOGIC HIERARCHY by cortical involvement: elementary, highly localizing signs (somatosensory/visual/auditory aura, focal clonic jerks, deja vu from entorhinal cortex) arise from primary/unimodal cortex and are reproducible by cortical stimulation; complex/less-localizing patterns (hyperkinetic motor behavior, altered consciousness, emotional signs) involve multimodal/transmodal associative cortex and distributed cortico-subcortical circuits. Qualitative localizing claims collated from cited studies (Tables 1-2): humming->STG+prefrontal (6/15 Hz, increased coherence); fear/defense behavior->orbitofrontal-amygdala loss of synchrony; impaired consciousness->excessive cortico-(thalamic) synchrony across temporal/parietal/frontal (Arthuis 2009 n=12; Lambert 2012 n=10 parietal; Bonini 2016 n=24 frontal); oro-alimentary automatisms->mediobasal temporal + insulo-opercular coherence (Aupy 2018 n=15); parietal: superior parietal lobule->vestibular symptoms vs inferior parietal->hyperkinetic (Bartolomei 2011 n=17); frontal rostro-caudal complexity gradient, 4 groups from central cortex rostrally (Bonini/McGonigal 2014 n=54); occipital 'occipital-plus' with temporal/parietal spread (Marchi 2016 n=29); insulo-opercular anteroventral->posterodorsal semiologic axis (Wang 2020 n=37); pure insula: anterior->hyperkinetic/speech-modification/viscerosensory vs posterior->pain/asymmetric tonic/focal clonic (Peltola 2020 n=11; Singh 2020 n=12). Notes most SEEG semiology series report ~20-50 subjects. Emphasizes methodological challenges (observer bias, categorization ambiguity of 'hyperkinetic'/'automatism', spatial sampling / 'missing electrode').

papers.cite: Luders H, Acharya J, Baumgartner C, Benbadis S, Bleasel A, Burgess R, et al. Semiological Seizure Classification. Epilepsia. 1998;39(9):1006-1013.
papers.design: Special Article - proposal of a Semiological Seizure Classification (SSC); framework, no empirical cohort
papers.notes: Proposes a Semiological Seizure Classification (SSC) based EXCLUSIVELY on ictal semiology (no EEG/other tests), tested >10 yr in selected centers. Ictal symptoms grouped into 4 spheres: sensorial, consciousness, autonomic, motor. Categories: (a) AURAS (subjective only): somatosensory, visual, auditory, olfactory, gustatory, autonomic, abdominal (closely related to temporal lobe epilepsy), psychic (deja/jamais vu). (b) AUTONOMIC seizures (objectively documented). (c) DIALEPTIC seizures - new term for altered consciousness independent of ictal EEG (typical dialeptic <20 s, ~3 Hz eye blinking; requires amnesia). (d) MOTOR: simple (unnatural, stimulation-like - myoclonic <400 ms; tonic >3 s; clonic 0.2-5/s; tonic-clonic; versive; epileptic spasm) and complex (natural-appearing but inappropriate - hypermotor [proximal limb/trunk], automotor [distal hand/mouth automatisms], gelastic). (e) SPECIAL seizures - negative/inhibitory (atonic, astatic, hypomotor, akinetic, negative myoclonic, aphasic). Somatotopic/lateralizing MODIFIERS: left/right/axial/bilateral-asymmetric/generalized refer to somatotopic distribution of symptoms; left/right hemispheric refer to hemisphere of origin. Qualitative LATERALIZING/LOCALIZING claims (no percentages given): dystonic posturing during a complex motor seizure, preservation of consciousness during an automotor seizure, ictal speech during automotor seizure, and postictal aphasia strongly suggest hemisphere of origin; automotor seizures may preserve consciousness particularly when originating from the NON-dominant hemisphere; example given - automotor + left-hand/arm dystonic posturing => right-hemispheric automotor seizure; akinetic/negative motor phenomena attributed to negative motor areas in mesial and inferior frontal gyri; negative myoclonic seizures generated in primary sensorimotor cortex; aphasic seizures from cortical language center; gelastic seizures common with hypothalamic hamartoma. Contains NO quantitative sign-specific localizing/lateralizing percentages (durations quoted are definitional, not localizing).

papers.notes: Textbook chapter (authored by Nancy Foldvary, DO) reviewing electrographic AND semiologic features of focal epilepsies. The bulk of its quantitative figures concern scalp/invasive EEG patterns and EEG lateralization (NOT clinical semiology) - e.g., ictal-onset pattern frequencies (sinusoidal 47%, repetitive discharges 39%, combination 15%, attenuation 11%; beta 50%/theta 20%/alpha or delta 10% each/no correlate 10%); lateralized rhythmic theta/alpha in ~80% of mesial-temporal cases predicting ipsilateral onset in >80% of TLE; postictal slowing predicting side in 96-100%; false lateralization 3-13% of MTLE; EEG correctly lateralizing 76-83% of temporal vs 47-65% of extratemporal seizures; scalp EEG detecting SPS 91% (motor 33% > nonmotor 15%), invasive 41-90%. These are EEG (not sign) metrics and were NOT extracted as semiology findings. Qualitative SEMIOLOGY localizing/lateralizing claims (no numbers): automatisms and dystonic posturing of the CONTRAlateral upper extremity early in seizure more common in mesial TLE, whereas early clonic activity of contralateral upper extremity or facial grimacing suggests neocortical temporal origin; motionless staring + oral/manual automatisms most common in mesial temporal seizures; SMA seizures - sudden bilateral asymmetric tonic posturing + vocalization with preserved consciousness; frontopolar - version, forced thinking, falls, autonomic; dorsolateral - tonic/clonic, version, speech arrest; opercular - gustatory hallucinations, salivation, mastication, swallowing, speech arrest; peri-rolandic - focal clonic, Todd's paralysis, epilepsia partialis continua; parietal SPS - contralateral somatosensory (paresthesia/numbness/pain/thermal); occipital spread patterns (infrasylvian->temporal automatisms; suprasylvian->mesial frontal asymmetric tonic posturing; lateral->focal motor/sensory). Lobar prevalence figures (not sign-specific): TLE ~two-thirds of adolescent/adult focal epilepsy; parietal 5-6%; occipital 8%. Extracted only the three clearly sign-specific frequency figures above (visual hallucinations 50-60% occipital; secondary GTCS ~50% mesial temporal; CP status 40% frontal). Year not stated in the extracted text.

Only procedural/service figures present (not sign-specific): 48-87% of patients undergo resective surgery after SEEG; average resective-surgery volume rose from 20 to 223/year (1990-2017); mean recording duration 8.5 days; average 10-13 electrodes/center (range 5-23); minimum skull thickness 2 mm; stimulation parameters (low freq 1 Hz, 0.5-3 ms, 0.5-4 mA, 20-60 s; high freq 50 Hz, 0.5-1 ms, 0.5-5 mA, 3-8 s). Notes that main SEEG limitation is sampling bias and difficulty precisely localizing functional/language areas.

papers.cite: Aron O, Jonas J, Colnat-Coulbois S, Maillard L. Language Mapping Using Stereo Electroencephalography: A Review and Expert Opinion.
papers.design: review and expert opinion
papers.notes: Review + expert opinion on sEEG language mapping via electrical cortical stimulation (ECS) - functional mapping, NOT seizure semiology; no spontaneous-seizure sign localization/lateralization data. Anatomy summarized: anterior language area (ALA)=posterior inferior frontal gyrus (BA44/45, pars opercularis/triangularis, +/-47/46); posterior language area (PLA)=posterior superior & middle temporal gyrus, angular & supramarginal gyri; basal temporal language area (BTLA)=fusiform/inferior temporal/parahippocampal gyri & occipito-temporal sulcus, ~1-9 cm (resected sites within 30 mm, mean 24.5 mm) from temporal tip. Key ECS/mapping figures (not semiology signs): ECS failed to localize anterior eloquent frontal cortex in 21% (Ojemann intra-op, n=117) and 30% (Schaffler extra-op ECoG, n=45); basic automatic-speech tasks miss ~2/3 of language sites; induced HFA vs ECS sensitivity only 8.9% (high specificity); frontal operculum expressive aphasia in 90% of responses, posterior temporal operculum positive in 11% of contacts (expressive aphasia in 2/3); BTLA strongly left/dominant-lateralized (strictly unilateral at individual level -> possible dominance marker); BTLA-positive-site resection -> ~60% early visual-naming decline vs 9% when spared (Abdallah 2021, recovers by 2 yr). sEEG judged valid/safe/reliable with good negative predictive value for ALA/PLA mapping.

papers.notes: Retrospective surgical cohort, Niguarda 'C. Munari' center; 135 confirmed SHE (9.4% of the epilepsy-surgery cohort; ~24% of drug-resistant frontal/frontal-plus and 4% of extrafrontal cases). 115 with clearly defined SOZ analyzed (20 excluded as widespread/undefined). SOZ (ground truth) defined by presurgical anatomo-electroclinical data incl. stereo-EEG (performed in 60%, 81/135), postop MRI, and Engel outcome. Within frontal lobe, semiology patterns follow a posteroanterior gradient SP1(posterior)->SP4(anterior). SP4 present only in frontal & temporal SHE (absent in operculoinsular/posterior) -> strongly localizes ventromesial prefrontal or anterior temporal region. Auditory & visual auras occurred only in extrafrontal SHE (visual->posterior cortex SOZ; auditory->temporal or operculoinsular SOZ) - qualitative (Table 2 counts: visual n=2 posterior; auditory n=1 temporal, n=1 operculoinsular). Interrater kappa: SP1 0.90, SP2 0.72, SP3 0.84, SP4 0.91. Other cohort figures (not sign-specific): interictal EEG abnormal 82% (111/135), localizing 48% overall but 93% in temporal SHE; MRI lesion 65% (88/135), probable FCD 52%; histopathology FCD type II 67%; Engel I 82% (104/127), Engel Ia 68%. Temporal SHE postictal-confusion rate ~3x other subgroups, similar to published TLE rate.

papers.cite: Hauser WA, Annegers JF, Kurland LT. Incidence of Epilepsy and Unprovoked Seizures in Rochester, Minnesota: 1935-1984
papers.design: Population-based retrospective incidence study (Rochester Epidemiology Project, 50-year cohort)
papers.notes: Pure epidemiology; NO seizure-semiology localizing/lateralizing figures (no contra/ipsi or sign->lobe data). 880 incident epilepsy cases over 2,003,357 person-years (plus 1208 first-unprovoked-seizure cases). Key figures: age-adjusted epilepsy incidence 44/100,000 person-yrs (male 49, female 41); partial seizures alone 57% of incident cases (age-adj incidence 25/100k) vs generalized-onset 17/100k; abstract text also states 60% manifested by partial seizures. Of partial epilepsies: complex 64%, simple partial 24%, uncertain 12%; secondarily generalized in 60% of partial cases. Two-thirds (573) idiopathic/unknown etiology; cerebrovascular disease 11%, neurologic deficits from birth (MR/CP) 8%. Cumulative incidence of epilepsy 3.1% through age 74, 4.4% through 85. First-unprovoked-seizure incidence 61/100,000 (33% higher than epilepsy). Contextual epidemiology only.

papers.cite: Sisodiya SM. Malformations of cortical development: burdens and insights from important causes of human epilepsy
papers.design: Narrative review
papers.notes: Narrative review of malformations of cortical development (MCD) as causes of epilepsy: FCD, periventricular heterotopia, polymicrogyria, subcortical band heterotopia/lissencephaly, dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumours (DNET), microdysgenesis. NO seizure-semiology sign localizing/lateralizing figures. Key claims: FCD found typically in frontal or temporal locations (may occur anywhere); >50% of FCD patients may become seizure-free after surgery; DNET 70-80% chance of seizure freedom post-resection; typical familial periventricular heterotopia caused by FLNA (Xq28) mutations in 83% of cases; MCD is the second most common cause of refractory epilepsy in adults among in-vivo-recognizable causes. Repeated theme: epileptogenic zone often more extensive than MRI-visible lesion; intracranial EEG improves resection completeness/outcome. Contextual/mechanistic only.

papers.cite: Jain P, Ochi A, McInnis C, et al. Surgical outcomes in children with bottom-of-sulcus dysplasia and drug-resistant epilepsy: a retrospective cohort study
papers.design: Single-center retrospective cohort (resection or MRgLITT) with invasive EEG
papers.notes: Retrospective pediatric cohort (n=41) of bottom-of-sulcus dysplasia (BOSD, FCD type II); ground truth = invasive VEEG (subdural grid+depth or SEEG) plus postoperative seizure freedom. NO seizure-semiology SIGN localizing/lateralizing percentages reported (data are lesion-localization, EEG and outcome). Lesion distribution: frontal 51.2%, pre/postcentral gyrus 19.5%, parietal 14.6%, insula 9.8%, temporal 4.9%; side Lt 56.1% / Rt 43.9%. Characteristic interictal EKG-like repetitive pseudoperiodic spike/sharp waves in 17/41 (41.5%). Ictal scalp EEG localized to lesion 68.3%; MEG clustered dipoles 62.5% (concordant 56%); FDG-PET focal hypometabolism 88.5% (concordant 80.7%). BOSD overlooked on initial epilepsy-protocol MRI in 48.8%. Ictal onset zone extended beyond MRI-visible BOSD in 23/34 (67.6%). Seizure freedom 32/41 (78.1%): lesionectomy 87.5%, extended lesionectomy 58.3%, lobectomy 100%(1/1), MRgLITT 75%. FCD IIB 53.8% vs IIA 41.0% (outcome similar). fMRI language lateralization (n=19): Lt in 14 (8 ipsilateral to lesion), Rt 1 (contralateral), bilat 4.

papers.cite: Suzuki H, Enatsu R, Kanno A, et al. The Involvement of Sensory-motor Networks in Reflex Seizure
papers.design: Single case report with chronic subdural intracranial EEG, ictal SPECT, and surgical resection
papers.notes: Single case (n=1): 18-year-old right-handed girl with medically intractable reflex seizures triggered by somatosensory stimulation to the RIGHT foot (also loud sounds), plus hypermotor and GTC seizures. Ground truth: two chronic subdural intracranial-EEG evaluations + resection + ictal SPECT. All localization was in the LEFT hemisphere (contralateral to the right-foot trigger): irritative and ictal-onset zones in left medial frontoparietal cortex (SMA, anterior and middle cingulate gyrus, paracentral lobule) and lateral posterior parietal cortex; ictal onset specifically at left middle cingulate gyrus, spreading to left paracentral lobule, pre/postcentral gyri, and superior parietal lobule. Ictal SPECT during provoked seizure: hyperperfusion in broad sensory-motor networks (dorsal column-medial lemniscus pathway, left thalamus, bilateral postcentral gyri and posterior parietal cortices, left SMA, left paracentral lobule) and left caudate-putamen. Histopathology: FCD ILAE type Ic. Second resection (left SMA, middle cingulate gyrus, part of paracentral lobule) produced remission of reflex and hypermotor seizures. No percentages/quantitative localizing metrics (single case).

papers.notes: Critical narrative review of epileptic aphasia. Key qualitative claims: isolated ictal aphasia is rare; simultaneous video-EEG is the gold standard to differentiate ictal vs postictal and aphasic symptoms from speech automatisms; reactive speech automatisms are NOT indicative of the seizure-onset zone; speech- or language-induced aphasic seizures originate in the dominant temporal lobe; dominant-hemisphere TLE is the most frequently reported epilepsy type for language disturbances. Privitera & Kim tested >10,000 seizures, vast majority without structural lesion (not a lateralizing metric). Tables 3-5 catalogue EEG patterns/etiologies/epilepsy types by reference only (no per-sign percentages).

papers.notes: Narrative review of epileptic ictal pain (cephalgic, somatosensory, abdominal). Key qualitative localization claims: IEH (ICHD-3 7.6.1) is ipsilateral to the ictal discharge and terminates with seizure cessation, more frequently with posterior/occipital foci; location of IEH pain did NOT have good localizing value for lesion/seizure focus (Cianchetti review). PSS network = SI, parieto-opercular SII, insula, cingulate gyrus, lateral thalamus; ESM/functional imaging suggest pain arises from insula + SII, not SI. Somatosensory pain typically contralateral to focus. Abdominal pain linked to temporal (amygdala) and parietal/operculo-insular foci. Resection of epileptogenic zone with symptom resolution = gold standard; video-EEG = gold-standard diagnostic. Many N values are denominators tied to rarity of the sign rather than direct localization statistics.
